refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (1) performance targets are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (2) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (3) history log captures all updates with reason, actor, and timestamp; (4) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → implementation is documented and queryable.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Derive performance targets (cycle time, energy, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context inputs
CR-NGHE-005.2: Record and version-control performance baselines with full metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, actor)
CR-NGHE-005.3: Maintain auditable history of all performance requirement updates and calibration changes
CR-NGHE-005.4: Provide traceability linkage from worksite inputs through derived requirements to implementation allocations

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized performance analytics engine that ingests worksite mission parameters, applies derivation algorithms (cycle time estimators, energy models, availability calculators), and outputs baseline performance targets stored in a versioned configuration database with audit logging
DP-B: Distributed performance derivation where each vehicle/subsystem independently computes its own performance targets from mission context, with periodic synchronization to a central registry
DP-C: Manual performance requirement entry by Site Manager with template-driven forms and optional automated suggestions from a lightweight analytics module, backed by a change-control workflow
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized performance analytics engine with versioned baseline database and audit logging

potential_sources: DP-A couples performance derivation logic to mission context interpretation; if mission context schema changes, derivation algorithms must be updated. Mitigation: define stable mission context interface (e.g., terrain type, payload mass, cycle count) and version it independently.
DP-A couples baseline storage to audit logging; if audit requirements change, storage schema must evolve. Mitigation: use event-sourcing pattern to decouple audit trail from baseline snapshot.

DP-A couples to external telemetry and tasking interfaces; if vehicle telemetry schema changes, derivation inputs may become invalid. Mitigation: define adapter layer between raw telemetry and normalized mission context.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is preferred because it centralizes derivation logic (avoiding duplication in DP-B) and enforces rigor (avoiding manual entry gaps in DP-C). Coupling is manageable via interface contracts and versioning strategy.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context can be reliably captured and normalized into a standard schema (terrain, payload, cycle parameters). Risk: If mission context is highly variable or poorly defined, derivation accuracy suffers. Mitigation: establish mission context data quality standards and validation rules.

Assumption: Performance derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy, availability models) are sufficiently accurate and maintainable. Risk: If models are black-box or poorly documented, they become difficult to audit and update. Mitigation: require model documentation, validation against historical data, and periodic recalibration.

Assumption: Centralized analytics engine has sufficient compute and network availability. Risk: If engine is unavailable, new performance baselines cannot be derived. Mitigation: implement fallback to cached models or manual override capability.

Assumption: Audit logging does not introduce unacceptable latency or storage overhead. Risk: If audit trail grows unbounded, storage and query performance degrade. Mitigation: implement retention policies and archival strategy.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A should be refined to explicitly specify: (1) mission context schema and validation rules; (2) performance derivation algorithm specifications (with references to models or empirical data); (3) baseline versioning and change-control workflow; (4) audit logging format and retention policy; (5) fallback/degradation modes if analytics engine is unavailable.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and provides the overarching performance management capability. Performance requirement derivation, baseline recording, and history management are system-level concerns that span multiple subsystems and require centralized coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission context and operational parameters (worksite inputs: terrain, payload, cycle count, operational policies) from Site Manager or planning system. Used as input to performance derivation algorithms.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems (actual cycle times, energy consumption, availability metrics) for validation and recalibration of performance models.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outputs performance-related safety alerts (e.g., if derived safety margins are violated or if actual performance deviates significantly from baseline). Feeds into Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides connectivity for performance baseline synchronization, audit log transmission, and remote access to performance history by Site Manager, Maintenance, and System Engineer.

module_containment_scores.notes: Performance requirement derivation and baseline management are system-level concerns that require integration across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Centralizing in NGHE Solution avoids duplication and ensures consistent traceability. Minimal diffusion: only system-level analytics and audit logging are needed at this boundary.

Vehicle subsystem could host local performance monitoring and telemetry collection, but derivation of system-level performance targets from mission context is not a Vehicle responsibility. Assigning to Vehicle would fragment the derivation logic and make traceability difficult. High diffusion risk: performance targets would need to be replicated across multiple vehicle instances.

Teleoperation subsystem is responsible for remote operation and control, not performance requirement management. Assigning to Teleoperation would couple performance derivation to tele-operation logic, creating unnecessary coupling. High diffusion risk: performance targets are independent of whether equipment is operated locally or remotely.

Safety Services could consume performance baselines and alert on safety margin violations, but deriving and recording performance targets is not a Safety Services responsibility. Safety Services should be a consumer of performance data, not the owner. Assigning to Safety Services would conflate performance management with safety monitoring.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Performance requirement derivation, baseline recording, and history management are system-level concerns that require integration of mission context (from Tasking), operational telemetry (from Vehicle and Teleoperation), and safety constraints (from Safety Services). These concerns are best owned by the NGHE Solution module, which has visibility across all subsystems and can enforce consistent traceability and audit practices.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution module becomes a monolithic 'god object' that owns too many concerns, it may become difficult to test, maintain, and evolve. Mitigation: decompose NGHE Solution into logical sub-modules (e.g., Performance Analytics, Baseline Manager, Audit Logger) in the next decomposition level, with clear interfaces between them.

Risk: If performance derivation algorithms are tightly coupled to specific mission context schemas or vehicle telemetry formats, changes to those schemas will require updates to the analytics engine. Mitigation: define stable, versioned interfaces for mission context and telemetry; use adapter patterns to decouple algorithm logic from data format details.

Risk: If audit logging is implemented naively (e.g., appending to a single log file), it may become a bottleneck or single point of failure. Mitigation: use event-sourcing or distributed logging patterns; implement retention and archival policies.

Risk: If performance baselines are derived but not actively validated against actual operational data, they may become stale or inaccurate. Mitigation: implement continuous validation and recalibration workflows; alert when actual performance deviates significantly from baseline.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate, but the module boundary should be refined to explicitly define: (1) sub-module structure (Performance Analytics, Baseline Manager, Audit Logger); (2) internal interfaces between sub-modules; (3) external interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) with schema definitions and versioning strategy; (4) deployment and scalability considerations (e.g., is analytics engine centralized or distributed?); (5) fallback and degradation modes.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (Centralized performance analytics engine with versioned baseline database and audit logging) directly addresses all aspects of the refined FR: (1) automatic derivation of performance targets from mission context; (2) recording and versioning of baselines; (3) maintained history with audit trail; (4) traceability linkage. DP-A is neither over-specified (it does not prescribe implementation details like database technology) nor under-specified (it clearly defines the functional approach). Coupling is manageable via interface contracts.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If performance derivation algorithms are implemented in multiple places (e.g., in Vehicle subsystem for local optimization and in NGHE Solution for global baseline), they may diverge and produce inconsistent results. Mitigation: enforce single source of truth for derivation logic in NGHE Solution; Vehicle subsystem consumes derived targets, does not compute them.

Diffusion Risk 2: If audit logging is implemented separately in each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), audit trails will be fragmented and difficult to correlate. Mitigation: centralize audit logging in NGHE Solution; subsystems emit events to a central audit logger.

Diffusion Risk 3: If baseline versioning is managed separately from audit logging (e.g., baselines in one database, audit logs in another), traceability may be lost. Mitigation: use event-sourcing pattern where audit events are the source of truth for baseline history.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate boundary for performance requirement derivation and baseline management because: (1) it has system-level visibility across mission context, vehicle telemetry, and safety constraints; (2) it can enforce consistent traceability and audit practices; (3) it avoids duplication of derivation logic across subsystems. The module boundary is clear and well-justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-005 is feasible via: (1) unit tests of derivation algorithms against known mission context inputs and expected performance targets; (2) integration tests confirming baseline recording and versioning; (3) audit trail validation confirming all updates are logged with metadata; (4) traceability analysis confirming linkage from worksite inputs through derived requirements to implementation allocations. Acceptance criteria (documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs, maintained baselines/calibration updates) are directly verifiable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-005 to NGHE Solution is clear. Sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.4) can be further allocated to sub-modules of NGHE Solution (Performance Analytics, Baseline Manager, Audit Logger, Traceability Manager) in the next decomposition level. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clearly defined and traceable to known interfaces.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine DP-A to specify performance derivation algorithm specifications (cycle time estimators, energy models, availability calculators) with references to empirical data or validated models. Include validation strategy for model accuracy.

Revision 2: Refine IM assignment to explicitly define sub-module structure (Performance Analytics, Baseline Manager, Audit Logger, Traceability Manager) and internal interfaces. This will support the next decomposition level.

Revision 3: Define mission context schema and validation rules as a separate design artifact. This schema is critical input to performance derivation and should be versioned and managed independently.

Revision 4: Specify fallback and degradation modes for performance derivation (e.g., if analytics engine is unavailable, what is the fallback behavior? Can cached models be used? Can manual override be invoked?). This is important for safety criticality.
Revision 5: Define audit logging format, retention policy, and archival strategy. Specify how audit logs are accessed by Maintenance/Quality and System Engineer for compliance and troubleshooting.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR explicitly identifies four sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.4) that address distinct concerns: (1) performance target derivation, (2) baseline recording and versioning, (3) history management and audit logging, (4) traceability linkage. Each sub-FR should be decomposed to the next level to define specific DPs and IMs. Additionally, the DP-A (Centralized analytics engine) is sufficiently well-defined to proceed with decomposition, and the IM assignment to NGHE Solution is justified and clear. Proceeding with decomposition of sub-FRs will refine the architecture and identify specific implementation modules (e.g., Performance Analytics, Baseline Manager, Audit Logger).
decision.justification_detail: The FR-DP-IM gates all pass, indicating that the current level is well-formed and ready for decomposition. The refined FR is clear and traceable to acceptance criteria. The selected DP is appropriate and coupling is manageable. The IM assignment to NGHE Solution is justified by containment analysis and avoids diffusion. However, the FR is explicitly non-atomic (is_atomic: false) and the sub-FRs are distinct enough to warrant separate decomposition. Proceeding with ZIGZAG_DOWN will refine the architecture and support detailed design and implementation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Derive performance targets (cycle time, energy, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context inputs
CR-NGHE-005.2: Record and version-control performance baselines with full metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, actor)
CR-NGHE-005.3: Maintain auditable history of all performance requirement updates and calibration changes
CR-NGHE-005.4: Provide traceability linkage from worksite inputs through derived requirements to implementation allocations

assumptions: Assumption 1: Worksite mission context can be reliably captured and normalized into a standard schema (terrain, payload, cycle parameters).
Assumption 2: Performance derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy, availability models) are sufficiently accurate and maintainable.
Assumption 3: Centralized analytics engine has sufficient compute and network availability.
Assumption 4: Audit logging does not introduce unacceptable latency or storage overhead.
Assumption 5: Performance baselines are actively validated and recalibrated against actual operational data.
Assumption 6: NGHE Solution module can be decomposed into logical sub-modules (Performance Analytics, Baseline Manager, Audit Logger, Traceability Manager) without creating unmanageable complexity.
open_risks: Risk 1: If mission context schema changes, derivation algorithms must be updated. Mitigation: define stable, versioned mission context interface.
Risk 2: If performance derivation algorithms are black-box or poorly documented, they become difficult to audit and update. Mitigation: require model documentation and validation.
Risk 3: If analytics engine is unavailable, new performance baselines cannot be derived. Mitigation: implement fallback to cached models or manual override.
Risk 4: If audit logging grows unbounded, storage and query performance degrade. Mitigation: implement retention policies and archival strategy.
Risk 5: If performance derivation logic is duplicated across subsystems, results may diverge. Mitigation: enforce single source of truth in NGHE Solution.
Risk 6: If baseline versioning and audit logging are managed separately, traceability may be lost. Mitigation: use event-sourcing pattern.
Risk 7: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic 'god object', it may become difficult to test and maintain. Mitigation: decompose into logical sub-modules with clear interfaces.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Mission context schema definition and validation rules. Confirm that worksite inputs (terrain, payload, cycle count, operational policies) can be reliably captured and normalized.

Evidence 2: Performance derivation algorithm specifications (cycle time estimators, energy models, availability calculators) with references to empirical data or validated models. Confirm model accuracy via historical data validation.
Evidence 3: Baseline versioning and change-control workflow specification. Confirm that baselines can be recorded with full metadata and that changes are traceable.
Evidence 4: Audit logging format, retention policy, and archival strategy. Confirm that audit logs are queryable and that retention policies meet compliance requirements.
Evidence 5: Traceability linkage specification. Confirm that worksite inputs can be traced through derived requirements to implementation allocations.
Evidence 6: Fallback and degradation mode specifications. Confirm that performance derivation can degrade gracefully if analytics engine is unavailable.

Evidence 7: Sub-module structure and internal interface specifications for NGHE Solution. Confirm that decomposition into Performance Analytics, Baseline Manager, Audit Logger, Traceability Manager is feasible and does not create unmanageable complexity.
